classes.name: tests.ptest_robust_lp.AFFUNCCONLP
documentation:
LP with single affine uncertainty in two constraints.

(N) max. ∑(x) + ∑(y)
    s.t. x, y ≤ 2

(R) max. ∑ᵢ(xᵢ + yᵢ)
    s.t. x + u       ≤ 2 ∀ u ∈ U  (Additive uncertainty)
         y ^ (1 + u) ≤ 2 ∀ u ∈ U  (Multiplicative uncertainty)
    for  U = {u | u ≥ 0 ∧ ∑(u) ≤ 1}

classes.name: tests.ptest_robust_lp.BUDGETUNCOBJLP
documentation:
LP with budgeted uncertainty in the objective.

The problem is constructed as follows:

1. The initial objective is to maximize the sum over an n-dimensional
   decision vector, with n even, which is bounded from above by a range.
   The unique nominal optimal solution is thus equal to that bound.
2. Uncertainty is added in the form of an adversary who has a total budget
   of n/2 that they may decrease the objective coefficients by, but each
   coefficient may be decreased by at most one to then equal zero. It is
   thus optimal for the adversary to select the n/2 largest elements in the
   decision vector and zero their corresponding objective coefficients.
3. The nominal optimal solution remains robust optimal, but its n/2 largets
   elements may be decreased without violating optimality as far as they
   remain the largest elements. A regulization term is introduced to
   encourage such a decrease. This makes the optimal robust solution unique.

classes.name: tests.ptest_robust_lp.BALLUNCLP
documentation:
LP with single ball uncertainty in objective and constraints.

The nominal problem asks for the nonpositive corner point of a two-wide,
n-dimensional box. The uncertain problem requires the point to have a unit
ball safety margin from the box. The objective is evaluated with respect to
the worst case location within that ball, which does not affect the optimal
solution but its value.

(N) min. ∑(x)
    s.t. ‖x‖_∞ ≤ 2

(R) min. max_{b ∈ B} ∑(x + b)
    s.t. +(x + b) ≤ 2 ∀ b ∈ B
         -(x + b) ≤ 2 ∀ b ∈ B
    for  B = {b | ‖b‖ ≤ 1}

classes.name: tests.ptest_robust_lp.DRUGS
documentation:
Ben-Tal et al. introductory example.

Implements the robust linear program introductory example found in
"Robust Optimization" (Ben-Tal, El Ghaoui, Nemirobvski, 2009).

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.NGP
documentation:
Geometric Program with Nonlinear Objective.

This is the "GP" test of the old test_picos.py. Note that the constraints
simplify to x + 2y = 0.

(P) min. log(exp(x - y) + exp(-x + y + log(2)))
    s.t. log(exp( x + 2y)) ≤ 0
         log(exp(-x - 2y)) ≤ 0

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.LGP
documentation:
Geometric Program with Linear Objective.

This is a reformulation of NLGP, with a linear objective function.

(P) min. t
    s.t. log(exp(x - y - t) + exp(-x + y + log(2) - t)) ≤ 0
         log(exp( x + 2y)) ≤ 0
         log(exp(-x - 2y)) ≤ 0

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.EGP
documentation:
Epigraph Reformulation of a Geometric Program.

This is an epigraph reformulation of NLGP.

(P) min. t
    s.t. log(exp(x - y) + exp(-x + y + log(2))) ≤ t
         log(exp( x + 2y)) ≤ 0
         log(exp(-x - 2y)) ≤ 0

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.ECP
documentation:
Exponential Cone Program.

In the following p is a scalar and Kₑ is the exponential cone. Note that
the constraint (C) is equivalent to eˣ ≤ p so that the solution is ln(p).

(P) max. x
    s.t. [p; 1; x] ∈ Kₑ (C)

(D) min. p·r + s
    s.t. [r; s; t] ∈ Kₑ*

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.LBSOEP
documentation:
Linearly Bounded Sum Of Exponentials Program.

In the following p is a scalar.

(P) max. x + y
    s.t. eˣ + eʸ ≤ p

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.EBSOEP
documentation:
Exponentially Bounded Sum Of Exponentials Program.

(P) min. x + y
    s.t. eˣ + eʸ ≤ eˣ⁺ʸ

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.SOPEP
documentation:
Sum Of Perspectives of Exponentials Program.

In the following p and q are scalars.

(P) max. x + y
    s.t. q·exp(x/q) + q·exp(y/q) ≤ p

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.SSNEP

class_variables.name: tests.ptest_exp.SSNEP.SOLUTION
documentation: This is an approximation of e^W(10) where W is the Lambert W function.

Single-Summand Negative Entropy Program.

(P) max. x
    s.t. x·log(x) ≤ 10

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.NEP

class_variables.name: tests.ptest_exp.NEP.XANDY
documentation: This is an approximation of e^W(5) where W is the Lambert W function.

Negative Entropy Program.

(P) max. x + y
    s.t. x·log(x) + y·log(y) ≤ 10

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.SSKLP

class_variables.name: tests.ptest_exp.SSKLP.SOLUTION
documentation: This is an approx. of 3·e^W(10/3) where W is the Lambert W function.

Single-Summand Kullback-Leibler divergence Program.

(P) max. x
    s.t. x·log(x/3) ≤ 10

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.KLP

class_variables.name: tests.ptest_exp.KLP.XANDY
documentation: This is an approx. of 3·e^W(5/3) where W is the Lambert W function.

Kullback-Leibler divergence Program.

(P) max. x + y
    s.t. x·log(x/3) + y·log(y/3) ≤ 10

classes.name: tests.ptest_exp.LOGP
documentation:
Logarithmic Program.

(P) min. x

classes.name: tests.ptest_wsum.WSUM_SOCP
documentation:
SOCP using Weighted Sums.

A constraint bounds a plus-minus-one-weighted sum over concave expressions:

1)  1 × <Power: u^(1/2)>
2) -1 × <1×1 Real Constant: 2>

The objective function of the minimization variant is an unweighted sum over
convex expressions:

1)  1 × <Quadratic Expression: xᵀ·2·x>
2)  1 × <Euclidean Norm: ‖x‖>
3)  1 × <Absolute Value: |t|>
4)  1 × <Squared Norm: xᵀ·x>
5)  1 × <1×1 Real Linear Expression: 10·u>
6)  1 × <1×1 Real Constant: 2>

A first maximization variant negates every summand explicitly, some of which
will register this negation internally and turn concave while others remain
convex and are stored with a weight of minus one:

1)  1 × <Quadratic Expression: -xᵀ·2·x>
2) -1 × <Euclidean Norm: ‖x‖>
3) -1 × <Absolute Value: |t|>
4)  1 × <Quadratic Expression: -xᵀ·x>
5)  1 × <1×1 Real Linear Expression: -10·u>
6)  1 × <1×1 Real Constant: -2>

A second maximization variant uses subtraction instead of addition, with the
following result:

1)  1 × <Quadratic Expression: -xᵀ·2·x>
2) -1 × <Euclidean Norm: ‖x‖>
3) -1 × <Absolute Value: |t|>
4) -1 × <Squared Norm: xᵀ·x>
5) -1 × <1×1 Real Linear Expression: 10·u>
6) -1 × <1×1 Real Constant: 2>

A third maximization variant negates the entire minimization objective,
which will result in a weight vector of all minus one.

classes.name: tests.ptest_sdp.SDP
documentation:
SDP with PSD Constraint on Variable.

(P) max. ⟨X, J⟩
    s.t. diag(X) = 𝟙 (CT)
         X ≽ 0       (CX)

(D) min. ⟨-𝟙, μ⟩
    s.t. J + Diag(μ) + Z = 0
         μ free
         Z ≽ 0

classes.name: tests.ptest_sdp.SDP2
documentation:
Projection onto the PSD Cone.

(P) min. ‖X - A‖
    s.t. X ≽ 0

(D) max. ⟨Y, -A⟩
    s.t. ‖Y‖ ≤ 1
         Y ≽ 0

classes.name: tests.ptest_sdp.SDP3
documentation:
SDP with Various Conic Constraints.

To establish correctness of the numeric reference solutions, we solve both
the primal and its dual problem to primal optimality. This is unlike most
other tests, where a "dual test" solves the primal problem to dual
optimality and values the dual problem with the dual solution of the primal.
In short, this test case does not test dual solution retrieval.

(P) min. ‖X - A‖ ≤ t
    s.t. tr(X + αI)^(0.5) ≥ β
         X ≽ 0

(D) max. ⟨Z, A⟩ + βμ - ⟨I, C + αH⟩
    s.t. Z + H ≼ 0
         ‖Z‖_dual ≤ 1
         [H, mu/2 I; mu/2 I, C] ≽ 0
         μ ≥ 0

functions.name: tests.manual.test_fileio.solve_file
documentation:
Load a problem from a file and solve it.

It seems that gurobi .lp files are not compatible with cplex
so we solve them with gurobi from the command line.

Similarly, mosek .mps files include a QSECTION for RSOC constraints
so we solve them with mosek from the command line.

functions.name: tests.manual.test_fileio.test_read_write_format
documentation:
Write a problem in a file using the desired writer.

Then, the problem is loaded again in CPLEX
and we compare PICOS and CPLEX optimal values.

functions.name: version._file_version_tuple
documentation:
Report the version stored in the version file as an integer tuple.

```
returns

A tuple (MINOR, MAJOR) or (MINOR, MAJOR, PATCH).
```

functions.name: version._git_version_tuple
documentation:
Report the version as reported by git as a tuple.

```
returns

A tuple () or (MINOR, MAJOR, PATCH, HASH) where all of
MINOR, MAJOR and PATCH and where HASH is a string.
```

functions.name: version._version_tuple
documentation:
Report a version tuple that is consistent between version file and git.

```
returns

A tuple (MINOR, MAJOR) or (MINOR, MAJOR, PATCH) or (MINOR,
MAJOR, PATCH, HASH) where all of MINOR, MAJOR and PATCH
are integers and where HASH is a string.
```

functions.name: version.get_version
documentation:
Report a version string that is consistent between version file and git.

:param int length:
    Level of detail.

:param str patchPrefix:
    String to prefix the PATCH bit with.

:param str hashPrefix:
    String to prefix the HASH bit with.

functions.name: version.get_base_version
documentation: Report the version in the MAJOR.MINOR format.

functions.name: version.get_full_version
documentation: Report the version in the MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format.

functions.name: version.get_commit_count
documentation: Report number of commits since the last release.

functions.name: version.get_commit_hash
documentation: Report the hash of git's HEAD commit.

functions.name: version.get_version_info
documentation: Report the version in the format of Python's __version_info__ tuple.

functions.name: version._repository_is_dirty
documentation: Report whether the repository has uncomitted changed.

functions.name: version.bump_version
documentation: Prepare the release of a new version.

functions.name: version.version
documentation: Load a parameter as a version tuple.
